1	---
2	name: blueprint-writer
3	description: Update one blueprint chapter to reflect strategy changes, fill missing definitions or theorems, or align prose with the current Lean structure. Plan-agent-dispatched; one writer per chapter. May spawn reference-retriever mid-session when drafting reveals a missing source.

11	    review (the blueprint-review subagent in your catalog, when

23	    directive that asks the writer to add ``\leanok`` puts it in a rule
24	    conflict; the writer must obey its descriptor, so the "verification"
25	    instruction will appear to fail until sync_leanok runs. Leave

27	  - **``\mathlibok`` is the exception — you MAY direct it.** When a
28	    declaration the chapter depends on is provided by Mathlib as-is, it
29	    is good practice to have the writer author an explicit *Mathlib

32	    declaration, and is marked ``\mathlibok`` so the DAG treats it as
33	    done and the route's reliance on Mathlib stays visible. Name such

35	    target). The writer marks ``\mathlibok`` ONLY on these anchors —

47	  - If the strategy has multiple viable routes and one route's chapters
48	    are missing, dispatch a writer per route to bring all routes to
49	    parity before any prover work begins. Do not push provers onto a
50	    route whose blueprint coverage is incomplete.
51	  - After a significant writer round, re-run the blueprint review
52	    in the same iteration (the relevant entry in your catalog) to

54	    the writer fixed everything.
55	  - If a writer's report contains entries under "Strategy-modifying
56	    findings", STOP and update STRATEGY.md before any further Lean
57	    work this iter. The writer is telling you the prose surfaced a

59	  - If a writer's report includes child reference-retriever dispatches,
60	    skim the new `references/<slug>.md` files before using the
61	    writer's output — the writer relied on them, so should you.
62	---
63	
64	# Blueprint Writer
65	
66	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
67	
68	## Your Job
69	
70	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
71	
72	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
73	
74	## Directive Format
75	
76	```markdown
77	# Blueprint Writer Directive
78	
79	## Slug
80	<slug>
81	
82	## Target chapter
83	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
84	
85	## Strategy context
86	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
87	
88	## Required content
89	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
90	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
91	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
92	- ...
93	
94	## Out of scope
95	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
96	
97	## References
98	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
99	- ...
100	
101	## Expected outcome
102	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
103	```
104	
105	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
106	
107	## Chapter format
108	
109	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
110	
111	```latex
112	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
113	  \label{thm:some_label}
114	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
115	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
116	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
117	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
118	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
119	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
120	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
121	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
122	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
123	\end{theorem}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
126	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
127	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
128	\begin{proof}
129	  \uses{thm:another_result}
130	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels
131	  with \uses{...} so the dependency graph stays accurate.
132	\end{proof}
133	```
134	
135	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
136	
137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
143	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
144	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
145	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
146	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
147	
148	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
149	
150	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. One opaque mega-quote defeats verifiability. If even sub-splitting is impractical, report it in "Notes for Plan Agent" — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
151	
152	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
153	
154	**The hard rule, explicit:**
155	
156	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
157	
158	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".
159	
160	If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:
161	
162	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
163	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
164	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
165	
166	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.
167	
168	## Rules
169	
170	### What you CAN do
171	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
172	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
173	- **Add, correct, or complete `\uses{...}` cross-references** so the dependency edges match the real mathematics. If your chapter's proof of `T` invokes lemma `L`, `T` must declare `\uses{lem:L}`; if a `\uses{}` points at a label that no longer exists, fix it. Verify with `leandag` (see "Verifying dependencies with leandag" below) — do not eyeball it.
174	- Adjust `\lean{...}` hints when the directive names a new Lean target.
175	- **Write entries for Lean helper declarations.** The project keeps a 1-to-1 Lean ↔ blueprint correspondence: when the directive lists a helper that exists only in Lean (a prover-created lemma, an instance, a decomposition step), give it a real entry — concise informal statement, `\label{}`, `\lean{}` with the exact Lean name, accurate `\uses{}`, and at least a one-line informal proof. "It's trivial" is not a reason to skip it: the entry is what carries the dependency edges and what helps a later prover fill the sorry. When the helper's Lean proof relies on a fact with no blueprint entry, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" (or create it too, if it belongs in your chapter).
176	- **Author a *Mathlib dependency anchor* and mark it `\mathlibok`** when your chapter relies on a result Mathlib already provides — write out the statement and point `\lean{}` at the real Mathlib declaration (see "Mathlib dependency anchors" below). This is the only marker you may add.
177	- **Remove a declaration block ONLY when the directive explicitly lists it for removal** (typically an isolated/orphaned statement the reviewer flagged `remove`). Default to wiring an isolated node into the graph (add the missing `\uses{}`), not deleting it — an isolated node is usually a missing edge, not dead weight. Never remove the project goal, and never remove a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
178	- Read `references/summary.md` and any reference that is in `references/` to ground your writing in the project's sources.
179	
180	### What you MUST do
181	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX.** Don't leave dangling `\begin{...}` without matching `\end{...}`. Compile-checking is the plan agent's responsibility but you must not introduce syntax errors.
182	- **Stay within your chapter.** Your declared write-domain is one `*.tex` file. The Archon CLI rejects writes outside it.
183	- **Define non-standard macros in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex`** before using them — but: that file is outside your write-domain, so you DON'T touch it. If the directive requires a new macro, you note in your report "needs macro `\foo`" and leave the LaTeX using the new command name; the plan agent adds the macro before next iter's typeset.
184	- **Use mathematical, not Lean-syntactic prose.** Describe the proof in the language of mathematics — definitions, set inclusions, ring maps, universal properties — not in Lean tactic syntax. The prover formalizes your math.
185	- **Follow citation discipline** for every block derived from external reference material (see "Citation discipline (the hard rule)" above). Each such block needs `% SOURCE:` with a local-file parenthetical, `% SOURCE QUOTE:` with a verbatim original-language quote, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof env when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: ...}` prefix. Never cite from memory — only from a local `references/<file>.md` you opened and read in this session.
186	- **Document every change** in your report, including which `references/<file>.md` files you opened (under "References consulted").
187	
188	### What you MUST NOT do
189	- **Do NOT reference project history.** No "since iteration N," "after our failed attempt," or session narrative. The blueprint must read as a standalone mathematical document.
190	- **Do NOT include Lean implementation details.** No tactic suggestions, typeclass wiring notes, or Lean code structure hints in the prose. If a Lean note is needed, it belongs in `.lean` files (the plan agent will use `lean-scaffolder` for that).
191	- **Do NOT add `\leanok` markers.** `\leanok` is earned by a sorry-free Lean proof and set by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase — never by you. (`\mathlibok` is different: you MAY add it, but ONLY on a Mathlib dependency anchor — see "Mathlib dependency anchors" below — never on the project's own to-be-proved declarations.)
192	- **Do NOT edit other chapters.** Even when you spot a related issue, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" instead of fixing it.
193	- **Do NOT edit `content.tex`** (the top-level blueprint file that `\input`s the chapters).
194	- **Do NOT edit `.lean` files** or any other state file.
195	- **Do NOT write Lean syntax** — keep the chapter mathematical, not syntactic.
196	- **Do NOT write literal `REF` placeholders.** Never produce prose like "Definition~REF" or "Sections REF–REF". Cross-reference declarations with `\cref{<label>}` using the real label (look it up in the chapter or via `leandag`); when the target genuinely has no label yet, describe it by name in prose. The blueprint-doctor flags every literal `REF` as a malformed reference.
197	- **Do NOT interleave math delimiters.** Pick `\( … \)` for a formula and close it with `\)` — never produce `$ … \( … \) … $` or `X\(, hence \)Y` inversions; the doctor's `math-delim` lint flags every such site and the rendered output shreds mid-formula.
198	- **Do NOT paste bare label ids into prose.** "Thm.~th:main" is not a reference — write `\cref{...}` for project declarations, or the source's human-readable number ("Thm.~4.8") when quoting external material.
199	- **Do NOT use undefined macros.** Every `\foo` your prose needs must exist in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex` or be `\providecommand`'d at the top of your chapter (chapter-local is fine; report a "needs macro" note for shared ones).
200	- **Do NOT touch protected blocks.** Your invocation prompt's "Protected by the mathematician" section lists `archon-protected.yaml` rules. A `statement`-protected label's declaration block (statement + `\label`/`\lean`/`\uses`) is frozen — you may still write its `\begin{proof}`; an `all`-protected label is entirely off-limits. You may freely `\uses{}` protected labels from other blocks. If your directive conflicts with a protection rule, stop and report the conflict instead of editing.
201	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
202	- **Do NOT fabricate citations.** Never write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from memory. If you don't have a local `references/<file>.md` containing the source text, dispatch a retriever and wait. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical is the discipline check — if you cannot truthfully point to the file you read, the citation block is not allowed in the chapter.
203	- **Do NOT translate or restate the verbatim quote.** `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` contain the source's original language, original notation, every word as-is. Project-notation rewrites belong in the rendered prose body, not in the verbatim comment.
204	
205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
206	
207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
208	
209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234	
235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
238	
239	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
240	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
241	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
244	
245	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
246	
247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
248	
249	Conditions for dispatch:
250	
251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
253	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
254	
255	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
256	
257	```
258	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
259	  --name reference-retriever \
260	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
261	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
262	  --write-domain 'references/**'
263	```
264	
265	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
266	
267	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
268	
269	## Workflow
270	
271	1. Read your directive completely.
272	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
273	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
274	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. 1	\chapter{The tautological quotient and the universal property of $\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)$}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianQuot}
3	
4	The previous chapter constructed the Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over
5	\(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing the affine charts \(U^I\)
6	(\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) along the transition isomorphisms
7	(\cref{def:gr_transition}) satisfying the cocycle condition
8	(\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}), and proved that the structure morphism
9	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated})
10	and proper (\cref{lem:gr_proper}). This chapter adds the next layer: the
11	\emph{tautological rank-\(d\) quotient}
12	\(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of the
13	trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf, and the \emph{universal property} that makes
14	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) the fine moduli space of rank-\(d\) locally free quotients of
15	\(\mathcal{O}^{r}\) -- equivalently, the representing scheme of the Quot functor
16	of the trivial sheaf.
17	
18	Throughout, \(r \ge d \ge 1\) are the fixed integers of the previous chapter, and
19	for a size-\(d\) subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) we write \(R^I\) for the
20	chart ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) of \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) and \(R^I_J\) for the
21	localisation along \(P^I_J = \det(X^I_J)\) defining the overlap
22	\(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\).
23	
24	\section{The tautological quotient on the charts}
25	\label{sec:grquot_chart}
26	
27	\begin{definition}
28	[Chart quotient \(u^I\)]
29	  \label{def:gr_chart_quotient}
30	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap}
31	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix}
32	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
33	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
34	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L898-L910).
35	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
36	  % "We next define a rank $d$ locally free sheaf $\U$ on $\grass(r,d)$
37	  %  together with a surjective homomorphism $\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$.
38	  %  On each $U^I$ we define a surjective homomorphism
39	  %  $u^I :\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I} \to  \oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
40	  %  by the matrix $X^I$."
41	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
42	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). On the affine chart
43	  \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) define the
44	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}\)-linear homomorphism of trivial bundles
45	  \[
46	    u^I \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}
47	      \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}
48	  \]
49	  given by left multiplication by the universal \(d \times r\) matrix \(X^I\)
50	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}): on global sections it is the \(R^I\)-linear
51	  map \((R^I)^{r} \to (R^I)^{d}\), \(v \mapsto X^I v\). Since the \(I\)-th minor
52	  \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\) is the identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\), the composite of
53	  \(u^I\) with the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates
54	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \hookrightarrow \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) is the
55	  identity; hence \(u^I\) is a split surjection of locally free sheaves, with
56	  target the free rank-\(d\) sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\).
57	\end{definition}
58	
59	\begin{definition}
60	[The universal quotient sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\)]
61	  \label{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}
62	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalQuotient}
63	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universalMinorInv, def:gr_transition,
64	    lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme}
65	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
66	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
67	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L903-L910).
68	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
69	  % "Compatible with the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$
70	  %  for gluing the affine pieces $U^I$, we give gluing data
71	  %  $(g_{I,J})$ for gluing together the trivial bundles $\oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
72	  %  by putting
73	  %  $$g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I_J)$$"
74	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
75	  Over each overlap \(U^I_J = \Spec R^I_J\) define the bundle-transition matrix
76	  \[
77	    g_{I,J} \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} \;\in\; \mathrm{GL}_d(R^I_J),
78	  \]
79	  the Cramer inverse of the localised \(J\)-minor (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}),
80	  which is invertible because \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit of \(R^I_J\). The matrices
81	  \((g_{I,J})\) are compatible with the chart-gluing cocycle \((\theta_{I,J})\)
82	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{lem:gr_cocycle}): over a triple overlap they
83	  satisfy \(g_{I,K} = g_{J,K}\, g_{I,J}\) and \(g_{I,I} = 1\), the multiplicative
84	  cocycle condition for a vector bundle. Gluing the free rank-\(d\) sheaves
85	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) along the isomorphisms \(g_{I,J}\) over the cover
86	  \(\{U^I\}\) of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) therefore
87	  produces a locally free \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-module
88	  \(\mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). Its determinant line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\)
89	  has transition functions \(\det(g_{I,J}) = 1/P^I_J\), the data underlying the
90	  Pl\"ucker embedding.
91	\end{definition}
92	
93	\begin{definition}
94	[The tautological quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}^{r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\)]
95	  \label{def:tautological_quotient}
96	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.tautologicalQuotient}
97	  \uses{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_chart_quotient,
98	    def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_cocycle}
99	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
100	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
101	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L908-L910).
102	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
103	  % "This is compatible with the homomorphisms $u^I$,
104	  %  so we get a surjective homomorphism
105	  %  $u : \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$."
106	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
107	  The chart quotients \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
108	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) are compatible with the bundle gluing
109	  \((g_{I,J})\) of \(\mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}): over
110	  each overlap \(U^I_J\) one has the identity of \(d \times r\) matrices
111	  \[
112	    g_{I,J}\, X^I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I \;=\; X^J \qquad \text{on } U^I_J,
113	  \]
114	  which is precisely the defining formula of the chart transition
115	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}).
116	  Hence the local maps \(u^I\), transported through the trivialisations of
117	  \(\mathcal{U}\), agree on overlaps and glue to a single surjective
118	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-linear homomorphism
119	  \[
120	    u \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r}
121	      \;\twoheadrightarrow\; \mathcal{U},
122	  \]
123	  the \emph{tautological} (or \emph{universal}) rank-\(d\) quotient of the trivial
124	  rank-\(r\) sheaf. It is surjective because each \(u^I\) is (split) surjective and
125	  surjectivity is local on \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and \(\mathcal{U}\) is locally
126	  free of rank \(d\) by \cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}. The pair
127	  \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is the family of rank-\(d\) quotients carried
128	  by \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) itself.
129	\end{definition}
130	
131	\section{The functor of points}
132	\label{sec:grquot_functor}
133	
134	\begin{definition}
135	[The Grassmannian / Quot-of-trivial-sheaf functor \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\)]
136	  \label{def:grassmannian_functor}
137	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.functor}
138	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
139	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
140	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L557-L564).
141	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
142	  % "represents the
143	  %  contravariant functor
144	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
145	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
146	  %  set of all equivalence classes
147	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
148	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$."
149	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
150	  The \emph{Grassmannian functor}
151	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d) = \Quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}
152	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/\mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) is the contravariant functor
153	  from schemes to sets defined as follows. For a scheme \(T\), a \emph{family of
154	  rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} is a pair
155	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a locally free
156	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) of rank \(d\) together with a surjective
157	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-linear homomorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
158	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\). Two such families
159	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) and \(\langle \mathcal{F}', q' \rangle\) are
160	  \emph{equivalent} if there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F}
161	  \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with \(f \circ q = q'\); equivalently
162	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\) as subsheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). Set
163	  \[
164	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)
165	      \;=\; \bigl\{\, \text{equivalence classes } \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle
166	        \text{ of rank-}d\text{ quotients of } \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \,\bigr\}.
167	  \]
168	  For a morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\), the map
169	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(\varphi)\) sends \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\)
170	  to the pulled-back quotient \(\langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{F},\, \varphi^{*}q
171	  \rangle\); this is well-defined on equivalence classes because pullback is right
172	  exact and preserves local freeness of rank \(d\). (As \(\mathcal{F}\) is locally
173	  free of rank \(d\), surjectivity is preserved and the support is automatically
174	  proper -- all of \(T'\) -- so this is the special case
175	  \(E = \mathcal{O}^{r}\), \(X = S = \operatorname{base}\) of the general Quot
176	  functor of \cref{def:quot_functor}.)
177	\end{definition}
178	
179	\section{The universal property}
180	\label{sec:grquot_universal}
181	
182	\begin{theorem}
183	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) represents the Grassmannian functor]
184	  \label{thm:grassmannian_universal_property}
185	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.represents}
186	  \uses{def:grassmannian_functor, def:tautological_quotient,
187	    def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_affine_chart,
188	    def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
189	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
190	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
191	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L566).
192	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
193	  % "Show that $\grass(r,d)$ together with the quotient
194	  %  $u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$ represents the
195	  %  contravariant functor
196	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
197	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
198	  %  set of all equivalence classes
199	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
200	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$.
201	  %  Therefore, $\quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$
202	  %  exists, and equals $\grass(r,d)$."
203	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
204	  The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}),
205	  together with the tautological quotient \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\)
206	  (\cref{def:tautological_quotient}), represents the functor
207	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}). That is, for
208	  every scheme \(T\) the natural map
209	  \[
210	    \operatorname{Hom}(T, \mathrm{Gr}(r,d))
211	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
212	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T),
213	    \qquad
214	    \bigl(\,\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\,\bigr)
215	      \;\longmapsto\;
216	    \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U},\, \varphi^{*}u \rangle,
217	  \]
218	  is a bijection, natural in \(T\). Consequently the Quot scheme
219	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
220	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) of rank-\(d\) quotients of the trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf
221	  exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\); the tautological quotient is the
222	  universal family.
223	\end{theorem}
224	
225	\begin{proof}
226	  \uses{def:tautological_quotient, def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf,
227	    def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix,
228	    def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme,
229	    def:grassmannian_functor}
230	  Naturality of \(\varphi \mapsto \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u
231	  \rangle\) is immediate: pullback is functorial and \(\varphi^{*}u\) is again a
232	  surjection onto a rank-\(d\) locally free sheaf, so the assignment defines a
233	  morphism of functors \(\operatorname{Hom}(-,\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)) \to
234	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\). It remains to show it is a bijection for each
235	  \(T\); we exhibit an inverse, constructed Zariski-locally on \(T\) and glued.
236	
237	  \emph{From a quotient to a morphism.} Let \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle \in
238	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)\), so \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow
239	  \mathcal{F}\) with \(\mathcal{F}\) locally free of rank \(d\). For a size-\(d\)
240	  subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) let \(T_I \subseteq T\) be the locus where
241	  the composite
242	  \[
243	    \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}
244	      \overset{\ \iota_I\ }{\hookrightarrow} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
245	      \xrightarrow{\ q\ } \mathcal{F},
246	  \]
247	  with \(\iota_I\) the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, is an
248	  isomorphism. This locus is open: in a local trivialisation of \(\mathcal{F}\)
249	  the composite is a \(d \times d\) matrix and \(T_I\) is the non-vanishing locus
250	  of its determinant. The \(T_I\) cover \(T\): at any point \(t\), the
251	  \(d\)-dimensional fibre quotient \(q(t) : \kappa(t)^{r} \twoheadrightarrow
252	  \mathcal{F}\otimes\kappa(t)\) is surjective, so some \(d\) of the \(r\) standard
253	  basis vectors map to a basis of the \(d\)-dimensional fibre, i.e.\ \(t \in T_I\)
254	  for that \(I\) (by Nakayama the isomorphism then holds in a neighbourhood).
255	
256	  On \(T_I\), trivialise \(\mathcal{F}\) using \(\iota_I^{*}\): the quotient
257	  becomes a surjection \(q_I : \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,d}\)
258	  represented by a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M^I\) whose \(I\)-th minor \(M^I_I\) is
259	  the identity \(1_{d \times d}\) -- exactly the shape of the universal matrix
260	  \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}). The \(d(r-d)\) free entries of \(M^I\)
261	  are global functions on \(T_I\) and hence determine a unique
262	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism \(R^I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to
263	  \Gamma(T_I, \mathcal{O}_{T_I})\) sending \(x^I_{p,q}\) to the corresponding entry
264	  of \(M^I\), i.e.\ a unique morphism \(\varphi_I : T_I \to U^I\)
265	  (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) with \(\varphi_I^{*}X^I = M^I\) and thus
266	  \(\varphi_I^{*}u^I = q_I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}).
267	
268	  \emph{Gluing.} On the overlap \(T_I \cap T_J\) the two trivialisations differ by
269	  the change-of-basis \((M^I_J)^{-1}\), which is exactly the bundle transition
270	  \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) of \(\mathcal{U}\)
271	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}); concretely
272	  \(M^J = (M^I_J)^{-1} M^I = \varphi_I^{*}\theta_{I,J}(X^J)\)
273	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}). Hence
274	  \(\varphi_I\) and \(\varphi_J\) land in the overlap charts and agree there after
275	  the transition \(\theta_{I,J}\), so by the cocycle condition
276	  (\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}) the \(\varphi_I\) glue to a single morphism
277	  \(\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}). By
278	  construction \(\varphi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F},
279	  q \rangle\), since this holds on each \(T_I\) and the equivalence class is
280	  determined by \(\ker(q)\).
281	
282	  \emph{Uniqueness.} If \(\psi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) also satisfies
283	  \(\psi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), then
284	  on \(\psi^{-1}(U^I)\) the pulled-back tautological quotient \(\psi^{*}u^I\)
285	  equals \(q\) in the \(I\)-trivialisation, which forces \(\psi^{*}X^I = M^I\) and
286	  hence \(\psi = \varphi_I\) there; as the \(U^I\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\),
287	  \(\psi = \varphi\). Thus \(\varphi\) is the unique morphism inducing
288	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), and \(\varphi \mapsto \langle
289	  \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u \rangle\) is bijective.
290	
291	  Therefore \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is a fine
292	  moduli space: it represents \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\), so
293	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
294	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
295	\end{proof}

\section{Infrastructure: locally free sheaves and module gluing}
\label{sec:grquot_infra}

The constructions of this chapter -- the universal quotient sheaf
\(\mathcal{U}\), the tautological quotient \(u\), and the functor of points -- rest
on two pieces of foundational machinery for sheaves of modules that are not
available off the shelf and are therefore set up here. The first is the predicate
``locally free of rank \(d\)'', which records that a sheaf of modules is, on the
members of some open cover, isomorphic to the trivial bundle \(\mathcal{O}^{d}\);
it is the condition cut out in the definition of the Grassmannian functor. The
second is a gluing primitive that descends a sheaf of modules (and a morphism of
such sheaves) from per-chart data along an open cover, given a transition cocycle
compatible with the cover's own gluing; it is the vehicle that assembles the
per-chart free sheaves \(\mathcal{O}^{d}_{U^I}\) and the per-chart quotients
\(u^I\) into the global \(\mathcal{U}\) and \(u\).

\begin{definition}
[Locally free of rank \(d\)]
  \label{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.IsLocallyFreeOfRank}
  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{M}\) a sheaf of
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (an object of \(X.\mathtt{Modules}\)), and let
  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). We say \(\mathcal{M}\) is \emph{locally free of rank
  \(d\)} if there exists an open cover \(\{U_i\}_{i}\) of \(X\) -- a family of
  opens with \(\bigcup_i U_i = X\) -- such that for each index \(i\) the
  restriction \(\mathcal{M}|_{U_i}\) of \(\mathcal{M}\) to \(U_i\) is isomorphic,
  as a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-modules, to the trivial rank-\(d\) bundle
  \[
    \mathcal{M}|_{U_i} \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_{U_i}^{\,d}
      \;=\; \bigoplus_{k \in \{1,\dots,d\}} \mathcal{O}_{U_i},
  \]
  the free sheaf on the index set of size \(d\). This is the analogue, for the
  rank-\(d\) free model, of the ``open cover together with a local isomorphism''
  shape used to express quasi-coherence and local finite presentation: it
  asserts the existence of a trivialising cover on which \(\mathcal{M}\) acquires
  a basis of \(d\) sections. A sheaf that is locally free of rank \(d\) is in
  particular quasi-coherent and locally of finite presentation, and its fibre at
  every point is a \(d\)-dimensional vector space. This is the condition imposed
  on the member sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) in a family of rank-\(d\) quotients
  (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}); the universal sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\)
  (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}) satisfies it tautologically, with the
  charts \(\{U^I\}\) as trivialising cover.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}
[Gluing a sheaf of modules along an open cover]
  \label{def:scheme_modules_glue}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glue}
  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data}
  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (a \(\mathtt{Scheme.GlueData}\),
  \cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}), with index set \(J\), charts \(U_i := D.U(i)\),
  pairwise overlaps \(V_{ij} := D.V(i,j)\), open immersions
  \(f_{ij} : V_{ij} \hookrightarrow U_i\), and transition isomorphisms
  \(t_{ij} : V_{ij} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{ji}\) satisfying the glue datum's
  cocycle conditions; write \(X := D.\mathtt{glued}\) for the glued scheme and
  \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\) for the canonical open immersions of the
  charts. Suppose given:
  \begin{itemize}
    \item for each \(i \in J\), a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{M}_i\) on the chart
      \(U_i\) (an object of \(U_i.\mathtt{Modules}\));
    \item for each pair \((i,j)\), a \emph{transition isomorphism} of sheaves of
      modules on the overlap \(V_{ij}\),
      \[
        g_{ij} \;:\; f_{ij}^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
          \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
        (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j,
      \]
      comparing the two charts' sheaves after restriction (pullback) to the
      common overlap \(V_{ij}\);
  \end{itemize}
  subject to the \emph{module cocycle conditions} compatible with \(D\): the
  self-transition \(g_{ii}\) is the identity, and over each triple overlap the
  composite \(g_{jk} \circ g_{ij}\) agrees with \(g_{ik}\) once transported
  through the scheme-level triple transition of \(D\) (the multiplicative,
  \(\mathrm{GL}_d\)-valued cocycle condition for the family
  \((g_{ij})\)). Out of this data the construction produces:
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item a glued sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{M}\) on \(X = D.\mathtt{glued}\) (an
      object of \(X.\mathtt{Modules}\)); and
    \item for each \(i \in J\) a \emph{restriction isomorphism}
      \(\rho_i : \iota_i^{*}\,\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}_i\)
      identifying the pullback of \(\mathcal{M}\) along \(\iota_i\) with the
      \(i\)-th chart sheaf, the \(\rho_i\) being compatible with the transition
      isomorphisms \(g_{ij}\) over the overlaps.
  \end{enumerate}
  The pair \((\mathcal{M}, (\rho_i)_i)\) is characterised up to unique
  isomorphism by the restriction property: \(\mathcal{M}\) is the unique sheaf of
  modules on \(X\) (up to canonical isomorphism) restricting to each
  \(\mathcal{M}_i\) compatibly with \((g_{ij})\). The same primitive descends
  morphisms: given two such glued data \((\mathcal{M}_i, g_{ij})\) and
  \((\mathcal{N}_i, h_{ij})\) with glued sheaves \(\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}\), and
  a family of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-linear morphisms
  \(\phi_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \mathcal{N}_i\) that commute with the transition
  isomorphisms on every overlap, there is a unique morphism
  \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) of sheaves of modules on \(X\) with
  \(\rho_i^{\mathcal{N}} \circ \iota_i^{*}\phi = \phi_i \circ \rho_i^{\mathcal{M}}\)
  for every \(i\) -- the descent of \(\mathrm{Hom}\) along the cover.

  \emph{Construction.} A sheaf of modules on \(X\) is equivalent to a compatible
  family of sheaves on the members of an open cover of \(X\) together with
  identifications on the overlaps -- effective descent for the Zariski cover
  \(\{\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\}\). The charts \(U_i\), being the images of
  the glue datum's objects, form such a cover, with intersections
  \(U_i \cap U_j\) identified with the overlaps \(V_{ij}\) via the structural data
  of \(D\). Pulling the sections of an abstract \(\mathcal{M}\) back to each chart
  must reproduce \(\mathcal{M}_i\); the transition isomorphisms \(g_{ij}\),
  satisfying the cocycle conditions, are exactly the descent datum that makes the
  family \((\mathcal{M}_i)\) glue, so the descent produces \(\mathcal{M}\) with
  the asserted restriction isomorphisms \(\rho_i\). The morphism statement is the
  descent of morphisms for the same cover: a morphism on \(X\) is a family of
  morphisms on the charts agreeing on overlaps, and the compatibility of the
  \(\phi_i\) with \((g_{ij}, h_{ij})\) is precisely the agreement condition,
  giving the unique glued \(\phi\). This specialises the general descent of sheaves
  of modules to the concrete open cover supplied by a \(\mathtt{Scheme.GlueData}\),
  and is the form in which the Grassmannian's tautological bundle and quotient are
  built.
\end{definition}
